content.text: React preserves the state of a component as long as it continues to occupy the same position in the tree with the same element type. The moment either the position changes or the type changes, React destroys the old Fiber — and its state — and starts fresh.

attrs.alt: State preserved vs reset based on position and type

content.text: Position is determined by the structure of the returned JSX, not by render-time conditions. React counts children left-to-right and top-to-bottom. A
occupies no position — it shifts the index of everything that follows it.
Rule 1: Same Position, Same Type → State Preserved
Same position, same type: state is
. React updates the component's props and re-renders it, keeping its Fiber (and therefore its
Rule 2: Same Position, Different Type → State Reset
Same position, different type: state is
. React tears down the old Fiber tree and builds a new one. The new component gets its initial state, and all effects run as if it mounted for the first time.

This rule is the reason you must never define a component inside another component's render function. The inner component has a new reference on every render, so its type appears to change every render — causing constant unmount/remount.
The most common source of unintentional state resets is conditional rendering that shifts subsequent children's positions. Using
does not matter for A itself, but it matters for siblings that follow A in the same parent:
are valid React children that render nothing but also take no position. Strings and numbers take a position and render as text nodes. This distinction is what makes
Component Defined Inside Render: The Type Instability Trap
Defining a component inside another component's render function is the most destructive form of type instability. A new function object is created on every render, so the
pointer always differs between renders even if the function body is identical:
Two Components of the Same Type at Swapped Positions

A subtle case: two instances of the same component type at positions 0 and 1. Swapping their positions reuses both Fibers and state even though the components have logically swapped — because both positions still have the same type:
Sometimes you want to keep state alive while hiding the UI. Two strategies:
Cheap components; state should start fresh
Expensive init; state must survive hide/show
Same as display; element still occupies layout space

The CSS visibility / display approach keeps the component mounted and its state alive, but the component continues to run its effects and respond to context changes even while hidden. For expensive subscriptions or intervals, this may be undesirable — prefer explicit mounting/unmounting with a key-based or state-based mechanism in those cases.
Effect Timing During Unmount and Remount

When a component unmounts due to a position or type change, React runs all of its cleanup effects synchronously before removing it from the DOM. When the replacement component mounts, React runs its layout effects (
) in the same commit that inserts it into the DOM.
value survives re-renders but not unmounts. When a component is unmounted (position or type change) and remounted,
starts at its initial value. This is the same rule as
— the ref's value lives in the Fiber, which is destroyed on unmount.

React DevTools shows the component tree with state values. When you suspect unexpected state reset, inspect the component in DevTools and watch its state as you trigger the condition. If the state jumps back to initial values, the Fiber is being destroyed. Check: does the element type at that tree position change? Does its position change because a sibling disappears?
Check element type at the position:
Is it the same function/class reference? If the component is defined inline, hoist it.
Is there a conditional sibling before this component that disappears? Switch to
Does the component have a key that changes when you don't want it to (e.g. using Math.random() or the item's text content which might be duplicated)?
In the Components panel, watch the component's state value as you trigger the condition. A jump to the initial value confirms Fiber destruction.

React preserves state when the same component type occupies the same tree position across renders. It resets state when either the type or the position changes. Position is structural — a null/false occupying no slot shifts siblings. Type is the exact function or class reference — an inline-defined component always looks like a different type. Use
to force state reset intentionally, and CSS / dual-rendering to preserve state while hiding an element.
Forms and the State Preservation Problem

Forms are the most common place where unintended state resets cause user-visible bugs. A form that resets its input values when an unrelated part of the UI updates is a sign that position or type rules are being violated.

The pattern of lifting form state out of the form component entirely is the safest: the form component becomes stateless, its parent owns the data, and neither conditional rendering nor layout changes can reset it.

Client-side routers (React Router, Next.js App Router, TanStack Router) remount the entire page component on route change. This is intentional — a new route is a conceptually new screen. State that should survive navigation must be lifted into a layout component that wraps both routes, or stored in URL search params, localStorage, or a global state manager.
keeps the current component mounted when a child route changes. The page component at the shared layout level preserves its state; only the

Context values do not reset on re-renders — they are not stored in Fibers. However, if a component that provides context unmounts and remounts (type or position change), the context's value returns to the Provider's initial value, and all consumers re-render with that new value.

) are resolved to their actual component type before reconciliation. Once the dynamic import resolves and the component renders, position/type rules apply normally. The Suspense boundary above the lazy component will show the fallback while loading, which unmounts the lazy component placeholder — its state is therefore always fresh when it first renders.
If you need a lazy component to preserve its state when it temporarily hides, use
instead of conditional mounting — the already-loaded component stays mounted.
React's StrictMode double-invokes the render function in development to surface side-effects in the render phase. It does not double-mount components —
runs once in StrictMode. But it does unmount and remount components in the Effects pass to verify cleanup is correct. During this remount, state
reset to the initial value for a brief moment before being restored. This is a development-only behavior and not representative of production.

Quick reference: same type + same position = update (state survives). Different type or position shifted = unmount + remount (state reset). Key change = unmount + remount regardless of type or position. CSS hide = stay mounted (state survives).
